title: Merck — Vaccines — briefing Q4-2025
html:
Merck Vaccines Quarterly Briefing — Q4 2025

Quarter: October–December 2025 Earnings reported: February 3, 2026

Governing Thought

Merck's Q4 earnings confirmed the worst-case GARDASIL scenario: FY2025 sales of $5.2B (39% decline), China revenue collapsed to $193M for the full year, and 2026 guidance assumes zero China GARDASIL revenue. The vaccine division's narrative has permanently shifted from "GARDASIL-driven growth" to "pipeline-driven transformation."

1. Financial Signals

Metric Q4 2025 / FY2025 Commentary
GARDASIL FY2025 $5.2B -39% from FY2024; worst annual decline ever
China GARDASIL FY2025 $193M Down from $3.5B in FY2024
2026 GARDASIL guidance $0 China Management assuming no China revenue in 2026
Merck 2026 outlook Missed expectations GARDASIL woes + Keytruda LOE concerns weighed on guidance
  • $193M China vs. $3.5B prior year = 94.5% decline — near-total China exit
  • Zero-China guidance for 2026 means Merck is planning for permanent structural change, not temporary pause
  • GARDASIL decline was the single largest drag on Merck's 2026 guidance miss
  • Bloomberg: "Merck's Full-Year Forecast Misses Expectations on Gardasil Woes"

What Merck management said on the Q4 2025 / FY2025 call:

  1. "We're managing through the China inventory situation." Management's language shifted from "challenging dynamic" (Q3) to "managing through" — a phrase that signals acceptance rather than optimism. The guidance of $0 China GARDASIL revenue for 2026 confirms the market has structurally shifted.
  2. "H2 2026 is our expectation" for China normalization — but this was heavily caveated. Management acknowledged that the domestic Chinese 9-valent competitor creates a permanently different competitive environment, implying that even "normalized" China revenue will be a fraction of the prior $3.5B peak.
  3. GARDASIL FY2025 at $5.2B (-39%) was the dominant vaccine narrative. Management attempted to reframe: "ex-China GARDASIL demand is stable" — but the absolute decline was too large to paper over, contributing directly to the FY2026 guidance miss.
  4. V940 5-year data (49% recurrence/death reduction in melanoma) was positioned as the pipeline upside for the vaccine division, but management was careful not to overweight a 2027+ readout against a 2026 revenue shortfall.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. "When will China normalize?" was the most frequently asked vaccine question. Management's "H2 2026" answer was met with skepticism — analysts noted the domestic competitor's lower price and established distribution make the recovery uncertain even on Merck's timeline.
  2. The tone on vaccines was unambiguously defensive. GARDASIL decline + Vaxneuvance's loss to Prevnar 20 meant every vaccine question was about problems, not opportunities. This creates a challenging environment for Merck's vaccine commercial team — leadership attention is on damage control, not growth investment.
  3. CDC single-dose HPV guidance was flagged by analysts as an additional headwind. If implemented, it could reduce US GARDASIL volumes by 50-67%. Management acknowledged the risk but provided no specific impact estimate.

2. Competitive Moves

  • Chinese domestic competitor: Gaining market share with lower-priced 9-valent HPV vaccine; Merck has no announced strategy to counter
  • CDC single-dose guidance: Formal recommendation expected — shifts US dosing from 2-3 to 1 dose
  • V940 5-year data: Moderna/Merck announced at January 2026 conference: 49% reduction in recurrence/death in melanoma Phase 2b — durable signal strengthening the Phase 3 investment thesis

3. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q4 2025

Company Brand Q4 2025 Revenue Key Signal
GSK Cervarix Marginal GSK pivoting vaccine investment to RSV and meningococcal; Cervarix immaterial
Pfizer Prevnar 20 ~$1.6B Prevnar 20 vs. Vaxneuvance — Pfizer winning pneumococcal share war
GARDASIL FY2025 $5.2B -39% YoY; China $193M (down from $3.5B)

GSK — Cervarix: GSK's vaccine franchise had a strong Q4 led by Shingrix and Arexvy, but Cervarix remained marginal. GSK's earnings commentary made no effort to position Cervarix against Gardasil — effectively acknowledging Gardasil's dominance. GSK is redirecting vaccine investment toward high-growth categories (RSV, meningococcal) where it can win, rather than competing in HPV where it cannot.

Pfizer — Prevnar 20 (~$1.6B): Prevnar 20 hit $1.6B in Q4, its strongest quarter. The Prevnar 20 vs. Vaxneuvance dynamic is becoming a competitive rout: Pfizer's pneumococcal franchise ($6B+ annualized) dwarfs Vaxneuvance (~$160M quarterly). This is Merck's most significant vaccine competitive loss and highlights why GARDASIL — despite its China decline — remains the irreplaceable pillar of Merck's vaccine division. Without GARDASIL's $5B+ revenue, the vaccine division would be commercially unviable.

Synthesis: The Q4 competitive picture reinforces GARDASIL's paradox: it faces no branded competition but is declining anyway. The threats are structural (China domestic competitor, shipment pause, potential CDC single-dose guidance) rather than competitive. Merck's vaccine portfolio health depends on GARDASIL stabilization and V940 pipeline success — not on defending against branded competitors.

4. Clinical/Regulatory

  • V940 5-year KEYNOTE-942 data: 49% risk reduction in recurrence/death for resected Stage III/IV melanoma — sustained benefit through 5 years
  • INTerpath-001: Fully enrolled Phase 3 in melanoma
  • INTerpath-009: Initiated enrollment in NSCLC (post-neoadjuvant Keytruda, non-pCR patients) — first patients in Canada
  • Merck's overall R&D pipeline includes vaccine adjacencies (V940 bridges oncology + vaccines)

5. What It Means for ZoomRx

  • GARDASIL tracking reframe: With $0 China guidance, the US franchise (~$2-3B) becomes the sole focus. CDC single-dose impact is the key US tracking question.
  • V940 5-year data validates research investment: Merck will increasingly need HCP awareness, intent, and barrier research for V940 pre-launch
  • Merck's 2026 guidance miss creates cost pressure — research budgets may be scrutinized; ZoomRx needs to demonstrate ROI clearly
  • VAXELIS remains the reliable recurring engagement while GARDASIL transitions

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
